does: This paper introduces MalSkillBench, the first runtime-verified benchmark for detecting malicious skills in AI coding agents. The benchmark contains 3,944 malicious skills organized across a three-dimensional taxonomy with 108 cells, plus 4,000 benign matched controls. The core contribution is a closed-loop Generate-Verify-Feedback pipeline that creates malicious skills and verifies their actual harmful behavior through Docker sandboxing with system-call monitoring and LLM-based judgment. The authors measure detection tool effectiveness against this ground truth and find that code injection-based attacks achieve 94.5% verification yield while prompt injection attacks only reach 75.8%, revealing asymmetric vulnerabilities in agent security. They supplement their synthetic pipeline with 703 in-the-wild malicious skills to ensure ecological validity.
connects: This work extends research on AI agent security and supply chain risks in machine learning systems. It builds on prior concerns about third-party dependencies in code environments and prompt injection vulnerabilities that have been documented in language model systems. The paper responds to the absence of rigorous, verified evaluation methodologies for malicious prompt and code detection—previous work relied on unverified wild-collected samples or simple classifiers without ground truth about actual malicious behavior. It combines insights from software security (sandboxing, system-call monitoring) with recent developments in agent capability research showing how coding agents increasingly integrate external tools and packages, creating hybrid code-instruction attack surfaces that traditional detection methods were never designed to evaluate.
enables: This benchmark enables systematic evaluation of detection tools and defense mechanisms against verified malicious agent skills, moving beyond speculation toward empirical security assessment. Future work can leverage the taxonomy and verified dataset to develop improved detection methods targeting the prompt injection vulnerability gap the authors identify. The runtime-verification methodology using Docker sandboxing and LLM judges could extend to other hybrid code-instruction environments beyond skills. The benchmark should support research into robustness of agent systems against supply chain attacks, development of safer skill distribution mechanisms, and formal verification approaches for agent-facing instructions. It also enables analysis of the relative security properties of different agent architectures and tool integration patterns, informing safer designs for future AI coding assistants.
